title: Why is Meta destroying its engineering organization?
body:
Why is Meta destroying its engineering organization?

Leadership at the social media giant has been on an AI-fueled rampage through its engineering org. We report what's happened

By Gergely Orosz, June 2026

For two decades, Meta had a unique, high-performance engineering org; right up until around April of this year. For the first 20 years of the company's existence, it had a "move-fast-and-break-things" culture, and in the early 2020s this shifted to a "move-fast-with-stable-infra" one. Engineers at the company were empowered to do good work, focus on impact, and to balance business interests with solid engineering.

But in the past few weeks, all that has changed, as if the leadership has been following detailed blueprints on how to demolish a proven, successful engineering culture in the most ruthlessly efficient way possible.

1. Meta's pre-AI engineering culture

I'd split Meta's engineering culture into two eras: "move fast and break things", and then "move fast with stable infra."

"Move fast and break things"

In the 2010s, Facebook's unconventional engineering culture had grown somewhat legendary in the tech industry, as the company went against conventional best practices and succeeded massively.

In 2012, when Facebook hit the billion-users landmark, the company produced a small physical book about its culture which was placed on employees' desks. Presented with retro propaganda design, it was dubbed the "little red book", co-opting the name of a famous volume of the thoughts of Chairman Mao, (1964).

At around 70 pages long, Facebook's version codified its engineering culture: speed, fearlessness, taking ownership, and thinking outside of the box.

Back then, mantras in Facebook's little red book were also in print across campus, and included: Move Fast and Break Things, Done is Better Than Perfect, Fail Harder, What Would You Do If You Weren't Afraid?, Every Day Feels Like a Week, The Wright Brothers Did Not Have Pilot Licenses, The Foolish Wait, Fortune Favors the Bold.

"Move fast with stable infra" culture

In 2022, I did one of the longest deepdives we've published on the topic of Meta's engineering culture. By then, things had evolved, and much of any former recklessness was gone, replaced by the principle of moving fast, but with stable infra. The culture was incredibly engineering-centric — much more than most of Big Tech. There was focus on individual impact, the lack of rigid processes, surprisingly little emphasis on testing, documentation or code comments. It was a founder-engineer driven company.

Facebook, as a product, has one of the most sophisticated auto rollout systems in the industry. Instagram has a battle-tested infrastructure where it was almost trivial to launch a new social network, Threads, with 100 million users served in its first week.

Engineers whom I knew inside the company are capable, motivated, and product-minded, and their work was appreciated. Engineers there felt they were working inside a profit center.

2. Investing in AI and pressing engineers to always use it

Meta has been the only company among the big five not to own a hardware platform or operating system. Stepping back, it looks as though Mark Zuckerberg has resolved not to miss a platform opportunity, after the company failed to build its own mobile OS or mobile phone during the 2010s.

When it became clear that AI would become a mega-trend in 2022, Zuckerberg assembled the internal FAIR group and a GenAI product organization and released a series of open-weight AI models: Llama 1 through 4. In June 2025, Meta acquired a 49% stake in Scale AI for $14.8B, and brought in Scale AI's CEO, Alexandr Wang to take over Meta's AI strategy.

Scale AI brings expertise in training data and labeling, and RLHF and fine-tuning. Wang seems to have a very broad reign to create training data, do data labeling and RLHF, using the labor of Meta's engineering workforce.

Problem #1: Tracking keystrokes and mouse clicks, with no option to opt out. In late April, Meta told engineers they were being enrolled into a system that tracks every keystroke and click, to produce training data for Meta's new AI. There's no way to opt out.

This month, Reuters reported that people's concerns are finally being heard. New controls will allow employees to pause the data collection for up to 30 minutes at a time and request exemptions.

Problem #2: 30-50% of engineers on core teams have been forcefully reassigned to data labeling and RLHF. Also starting in late April, product engineering teams received a mandate from above, whereby 30-50% of engineers were to leave the team and join the ADO org (Agent Data Optimisation).

"Forceful" reassignment is very relevant because of Meta's traditional engineering culture. Engineers were not hired for a specific team. They were hired to the company. During a 6-week bootcamp, new hires chose a team. Internal transfers were easy, and often initiated by engineers. And then, out of the blue, they're assigned to a division where the impact is not clear, the work is menial, and doing it too long will surely hurt their career prospects.

The data labeling work involves coming up with tasks for AI, writing tests, packaging into Docker containers using Harbor framework, then reading AI-generated code and giving feedback. This work is not easy to do — and you need good software engineers to do it — but it gets repetitive really quickly.

Infrastructure and security teams were hit especially hard by reassignments. One engineer told me that the whole situation feels like the movie The Hunger Games, when tributes are randomly selected and removed from their environment. Except at Meta, many more folks are being affected, with between three and five from a 10-person team going from building products used by hundreds of millions, to giving human feedback on AI-generated GitHub repos.

Around 6,500 people are in the ADO org, more than at OpenAI and Anthropic. Roughly four to five thousand are software engineers. Meta has around 25,000 engineers, meaning one in every 5-6 software engineers may now find themselves doing data labeling full time.

3. Core engineering folks feel treated like trash

Problem #3: a month-long waiting game, stoking fear across the company. On 20 April, Reuters reported that Meta planned to lay off 10% of staff in a month's time. There was a period of four weeks when everyone knew they could be unemployed very soon.

Problem #4: Performance review is hyper-aggressive at Meta. The internal performance review process, PSC, is very stringent. Managers "fight" over the pay packets of their employees by "knocking down" the packet of engineers on other teams. It's common to weaponize metrics.

Problem #5: tokens are measured as part of perf, so devs aggressively optimize for it. When layoffs were confirmed, engineers also learned that managers shall inspect token count during perf reviews. This raised worries that those with low token counts might be marked as underperformers.

So, what is the natural reaction? They started using AI tools for the sake of generating more tokens. Meta employees used a total of 60.2 trillion AI tokens in 30 days. If this was charged at Anthropic's API prices, it would cost $900M.

The biggest problem: people stop caring about real work and focus on performative work. Shake up tracking keyboards, data labeling, layoffs, metric optimization, and token measurement — and what do you get? Everyone overuses AI to boost their personal stats. An engineering workforce that pretends to work with as much AI, and as little human input, as possible. And every longer-tenured engineer is seeking a new job.

Fresh data confirms that starting in May, a lot more engineers at Meta are looking for an "out." Signups to interviewing.io from Meta show a massive jump in May versus the year before.

To its credit, Meta has given out generous retention equity packages to several key engineers. Still, one engineer who got an equity top-up said this approach helped him decide to leave as soon as possible because he feels bitter about the lack of autonomy.

4. Most embarrassing-ever outage

Meta's core infra and security teams have suddenly found themselves severely understaffed. Most folks are pushing AI-generated code merged with AI-only reviews, without paying much attention to quality.

Two weeks ago, on 30 May, the most embarrassing outage in Meta's history happened. Instagram accounts, including high profile ones like the Obama White House account, were hacked. The attacker just needed your username, hopped on a VPN near your city, and told the Meta support AI that the account was hacked. The AI sent verification codes to the attacker's email, and the platform handed over full ownership. The first proper zero auth password reset anyone has seen in production.

From talking with folks inside Meta, AI was at the heart of this outage. AI-generated, AI-reviewed code, and security teams being gutted were together the cause. Instagram's Trust and Safety Team lost around 50% of its staff. AI-generated changes that saw no human input were very common during the last two months.

Meta's Chief Security Officer resigned the very next day. The CISO might have stepped down if they warned against the Security org being gutted but were then ignored.

5. Internal mess

Someone interrupted a livestreamed, employee-only presentation at Meta with an expletive-filled outburst about "being the company's bitch," asking people to write to a specific Meta AI executive and "tell him that he's a piece of shit." The incident reflects growing frustration inside the Applied AI team.

One employee called it "literally the gulag." "You have zero purpose in life all of a sudden, you barely interact with anyone, you just have these tasks every week."

Meta's Chief Product Officer, Chris Cox, reportedly admitted to staff that Meta's upper leadership created the mess, calling it "difficult" and "brutal."

6. Self-inflicted wounds

Engineers point the finger at two individuals: Mark Zuckerberg and Alexandr Wang. Everything Meta is doing is taken from the Scale AI playbook, and that surely comes from Wang: mandatory keystroke tracking, forced data labeling with 4,500+ engineers, taking away the best engineers from the heart of the business.

Meta's leadership is now trying to undo all the damage. CTO Andrew Bosworth admitted to staff that the AI reorg was atrocious and committed to better communication. It looks obvious that Zuckerberg doesn't care how engineers feel about the massive changes.

Based on all I've learned, Meta's engineering culture is dead because leadership has made it clear that engineering at the company is a cost center.

7. Is "AI psychosis" just a Meta issue?

Mitchell Hashimoto, creator of Ghostty and founder of HashiCorp, says he is seeing similar behavior by other founders. "I strongly believe there are entire companies right now under heavy 'AI psychosis' and it's impossible to have rational conversations about it with them."

He draws parallels to the great MTBF vs MTTR reckoning during the transition to cloud automation. "Psychosis folks operate under an almost absolute MTTR is all you need mentality: it's fine to ship bugs because the agents will fix them so quickly and at a scale humans can't do!"

"You can automate yourself into a very resilient catastrophe machine. Systems can appear healthy by local metrics while globally becoming incomprehensible."

The takeover outage at Instagram was exactly like this: the team dropped the quality bar for AI-generated and AI-reviewed code, expecting they could recover quickly from failures. And they did recover — after the damage was done.

Takeaways

If you're in a leadership position and feeling the temptation to make drastic org changes for AI-related reasons, take a deep breath and see where it left Meta. If you're an engineer at a company whose leadership is over-indexing on AI, consider forwarding this article as additional context. If you're hiring standout engineers, it's never been easier to get talent from Meta than right now.

The old mantra of "move fast and break things" has now extended to Meta's engineering org itself.
